Configuring the Uniface Router on iSeries

After installation, the Uniface Router runs out of the box, either by starting the subsystem or, if you elected not to create a subsystem, by calling the STARTUP program. However, in a typical Uniface environment there are multiple UST's configured to handle different types of requests. These user-specific UST's must be manually configured after installation.

To configure the Uniface Router, you need to edit its assignment file. On iSeries, this is member UROUTER in source physical file ASN, which is created during installation.

The Uniface Router assignment file member UROUTER contains the following:

[SETTINGS]
;$putmess_log_file = UNIFxxxx/rout%j.log
[SERVERS] ; Specify USTs here:
default = userver -maxidle=3m 
; For Uniface WebApplication Server
wasv = userver -maxreq=1000 -asn=wasv.asn

The [SERVERS] section defines the USTs (Uniface Server Type) for the default Uniface Server (default) and the Uniface Web Application Server (wasv), plus the command line parameters for each server. You can add more entries in the [SERVERS] section to specify Uniface Servers with different parameters.

The UST can be added to the path in the client assignment file that accesses this Router. This instructs the Uniface Router to start a Uniface Server using the userver program with the indicated parameters. If no UST is specified, the Uniface Router uses the default UST to start a Uniface Server of type ANY.

To add the UST, add a pipe (|) followed by a UST immediately after the password in the client’s $DB2 setting. For example:, to add the wasv UST:

$WEB TCP: iSeriesMachineName + Port | UserName | Password |wasv

A Uniface Server (USERVER) will be started (by UROUTER) with the command line parameters -maxreq=1000 and -asn=wasv.asn. In this example, wasv.asn is the server’s assignment file, which contains assignment settings for the Uniface Web Application Server.

  1. In the [SERVERS] section, define the USTs (Uniface Server Type) of the Uniface Servers to be accessed by the Uniface Router. For more information, see Define Uniface Server Types (USTs).

    The Uniface Router also inserts a number of parameters of its own. You can add or remove any flag or argument that the Uniface Server accepts.

  2. Optionally, in the [PRE_START] section, define Uniface Servers that the Uniface Router should automatically pre-start. For more information, see Pre-Start Uniface Servers.
  3. In the [SETTINGS] section, specify the default settings to constrain the number of clients, Uniface Servers, and Uniface Router Monitors.
    • $MAX_SERVERS—the maximum number of Uniface Servers that the Uniface Router can start.
    • $MAX_CLIENTS—maximum number of clients that you want the Uniface Router to accept.
    • $MAX_MONS—maximum number of Uniface Router Monitors that you want the Uniface Router to accept.
  4. If necessary, change the startup parameters. For more information, see Change the Uniface Router Startup Parameters.
  5. Save your changes and restart the Uniface Router. For more information, see Start or Stop the Uniface Router on iSeries.
    Any changes you made now take effect.
  6. For each Uniface client application that receives messages from another Uniface application (client or server) via the Uniface Router, ensure that is registered with that Uniface Router. For more information, see Registering Clients with the Uniface Router.
Related Links
  • Uniface Router

    The Uniface Router is a multi-threaded process (urouter) responsible for interprocess communication in Uniface applications. It listens for requests from Uniface client applications and routes them to the appropriate Uniface Server, and it passes messages between various Uniface applications.
